video_title: How To Trade The AI Productivity Boom | Weekly Roundup
gist: Three macro/crypto traders argue the AI productivity boom is real but policy-managed: the Fed won't hike despite Taylor-rule signals, negative real rates are 'here for good,' and the market is a centralized, vol-suppressed melt-up with extreme call-skew that they'd rather watch than chase. They are structurally bearish crypto (broken token value-capture), wary of an Iran oil-supply stagflation shock, and positioned for a sector rotation out of semis rather than an outright crash.

themes.theme: Fed policy & negative real rates regime

insights.claim: Taylor-rule and DB R-star models prescribe rate HIKES given Fed funds is below policy-rule levels, but the panel does not expect the Fed to hike at all because this is 'wartime policy' managing a massive deficit and ~$1 trillion of interest expense.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: Curve is starting to price a first hike by end-2026; fading that and positioning for continued financial repression / no-hike is the base case.
insights.quote: by the end of 2026, we might even get the first hike

insights.claim: The real policy stance is far more stimulative than rate cuts alone: suppressing oil prices, suppressing yields, yen/dollar currency management and balance-sheet/RMP support of the long end together raise the odds an energy price shock seeps from headline into core inflation.
insights.speaker: guest, former gold-fund PM
insights.so_what: Watch core inflation pass-through; the trade is removal of stimulus, not hikes — but until that happens stimulus keeps risk assets bid.
insights.quote: all of the other stimulative measures they're doing like suppressing oil prices, uh, suppressing yields, currency manipulation with the yen and dollar, all of that stuff is stimulative

insights.claim: Inflation has run above the Fed's 2% target for 60+ months, yet Powell has been 'unbelievably dovish' and balance-sheet support of the long end has been more stimulative than cuts would have been.
insights.speaker: guest, former gold-fund PM
insights.so_what: Persistent above-target inflation plus dovish bias supports owning real/productive assets over cash and bonds.
insights.quote: inflation's been above the Fed's target for 60 plus months

insights.claim: The long-term policy endgame is to fiscally grow out of the debt with negative real rates 'here for good,' implying the market is correctly front-running a deliberate inflate-away regime.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: Negative real rates argue for staying long hard/productive assets and short duration / cash as a structural posture.
insights.quote: we're going to fiscally grow our way out of this and negative real rates are going to be here for good

insights.claim: Mention of Kevin Warsh as 'slightly more hawkish' is offset by Trump-appointee pressure, so even a leadership change is unlikely to deliver materially tighter policy.
insights.speaker: guest, former gold-fund PM
insights.so_what: Don't position for a hawkish Fed regime change; personnel signaling on footprint-reduction has not translated into action.
insights.quote: I get that Worsh is maybe slightly more hawkish, but then you have the offsetting factor of the Trump appointee and pressure

themes.theme: Vol mechanics, positioning & how to trade the melt-up

insights.claim: VIX is sub-16 after a spike near 40, and the panel refuses to add risk into euphoric grind-up tape with extreme positioning, preferring to wait for better prices.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: Tactical traders should treat sub-16 VIX + euphoria as a reason to harvest, not chase; mean-reversion risk is rising.
insights.quote: I hate taking risk when things grind up every day and the VIX is, you know, sub 16 after being at what was it like 40

insights.claim: Big semiconductor single-stock call/put skew sits in the 98th percentile — investors are aggressively bidding calls with essentially no downside protection bought, the mirror image of the fear seen during the Iran shock.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: 98th-percentile call skew with no hedges flags an asymmetric short-term downside; cheap puts make defined-risk hedges attractive.
insights.quote: It's in like the 98th percentile meaning like people are really bidding up calls to to puts. There's no downside protection being bought.

insights.claim: Low VIX is structurally manufactured: retail/momentum players go 'balls long' single-stock calls (raising single-stock vol) while quant funds short index vol as a hedge, and falling implied correlation lets systematic funds keep buying what's working.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: The dispersion/low-correlation regime is self-reinforcing but can violently unwind like the 2024 carry trade — a key risk-trigger to monitor.
insights.quote: the quant funds are shorting you know index volatility. So that's why we see you know the VIX is so low is that's that's their hedge.

insights.claim: S&P one-month 25-delta put/call skew is in the 4th percentile (puts extraordinarily cheap vs calls), per a Clear Street contact.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: 4th-percentile put pricing makes index downside protection unusually cheap — a low-cost hedge or tail trade.
insights.quote: it's in 25 delta implied V spread for puts relative to calls in the fourth percentile, which is like super super cheap on on the put side

insights.claim: Retail using Robinhood to buy YOLO call options is now a primary driver of gamma squeezes because pensions/endowments/boomer holders have 'choked the float' of the equity market.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: Thin float plus retail call-buying means dealer gamma dynamics, not fundamentals, drive marginal price action — trade the flows.
insights.quote: they're figuring out how to use Robin Hood and and basically buy call options to artificially create gamma squeezes

insights.claim: The panel is not outright bearish but expects a 'massive sectoral rotation' / baton-pass out of semis rather than an index crash, because credit spreads are too tight for a 'volmageddon.'
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: Position for intra-market rotation (rotate out of semis, watch squeezes elsewhere) over the next few weeks rather than net-short the index.
insights.quote: there will be a baton passing at some point over the next few weeks I think from semis and that doesn't even need to be market down

insights.claim: Snowflake-driven short squeeze in software is cited as a template for how deleveraging in one frothy sector can spark squeezes elsewhere.
insights.speaker: guest, former gold-fund PM
insights.so_what: Single-name squeezes can cascade across crowded sectors — watch for follow-on squeezes when one frothy name deleverages.
insights.quote: we saw a lot of the software stuff like snowflake caused a big squeeze in in software

themes.theme: AI productivity boom: real but socially destabilizing

insights.claim: The AI productivity boom is judged to be real and 'working,' creating monstrous earnings winners, but it is breaking the social contract during a brutal transition for displaced workers.
insights.speaker: guest, former gold-fund PM
insights.so_what: Stay long the AI/productivity earnings winners while pricing rising political-backlash risk as a second-order tail.
insights.quote: we're seeing a massive productivity boom... the one piece where I think it's failing is it's not it's breaking the social contract

insights.claim: If the dislocation isn't addressed politically, the likely outcome is AOC/Mamdani-style left-populist redistributive policy rather than hands-off conservative governance — a structural risk to asset-holders.
insights.speaker: guest, former gold-fund PM
insights.so_what: Long-horizon investors should price in higher future taxes/redistribution and political risk to concentrated capital.
insights.quote: you're probably more likely with the AOC and Mumanis than you are with you know handsoff LZ fair uh conservative government

insights.claim: A speaker openly entertains that AI could be so productive (hypothetical ~15% national GDP growth) that it ushers in an entirely new economy in which a store-of-value asset like Bitcoin loses its purpose.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: A genuinely transformative AI-growth scenario is bearish the macro case for non-yielding store-of-value assets — a contrarian risk to the Bitcoin thesis.
insights.quote: what is the role of crypto if you're you have national GDP P growth of 15%. Do you need a store of value?

insights.claim: There is 'a clear bid for productive assets,' which is exactly why non-productive/no-cashflow tokens are being left behind.
insights.speaker: guest (Felix/Quinn)
insights.so_what: Favor cash-flowing productive assets over speculative non-yielding ones in this regime; it explains crypto underperformance.
insights.quote: there's there's a clear bid for productive assets right now

themes.theme: Crypto bear case & broken token value-capture

insights.claim: The only parts of crypto leaning into the 'productive asset' theme — stablecoin adoption and payment rails — have no investable token, so usage rises while token prices capture no value (e.g., Ethereum network usage up, token price decoupled).
insights.speaker: guest (Felix/Quinn)
insights.so_what: Adoption is not a buy signal for the underlying token; the edge is in the rails/usage, not ETH or most L1 tokens.
insights.quote: you see more and more people using Ethereum for example the network but it really does not correlate to the to the price of their token

insights.claim: Bankless co-founder David Hoffman, an OG ETH believer, sold his Ethereum this week, calling the network fine but the asset itself 'uninvestable' — a capitulation signal among long-time holders.
insights.speaker: guest (Felix/Quinn)
insights.so_what: OG holders capitulating on ETH is either a deepening bear signal or a sentiment-washout marker to watch for a bottom.
insights.quote: David Hoffman from Bank list recently sold his Ethereum this week and he's like I'm bullish on the network... the the the asset itself is uninvestable

insights.claim: Crypto Treasury companies ('DATs') bought roughly $15 billion of Bitcoin and ETH this year, yet both assets are down massively YTD — characterized as financial engineering propping up artificial demand and scamming retail.
insights.speaker: guest, former gold-fund PM
insights.so_what: Massive corporate buying failing to lift price signals weak underlying liquidity — a structural red flag for BTC/ETH near-term.
insights.quote: these guys have bought 15 trillion 15 billion dollars of Bitcoin and ETH this year, and both assets are down massively year to date

insights.claim: Because equities are propped by vol-suppression, passive flows and the systematic bid, crypto lacks the same support mechanism and is therefore in a bear market with poor under-the-surface liquidity.
insights.speaker: guest, former gold-fund PM
insights.so_what: Crypto is the wrong vehicle for this macro regime; the systematic/passive bid that supports stocks doesn't exist for it.
insights.quote: it makes sense that crypto is lagging... it's just not the right market environment and macro conditions for it to do well

insights.claim: Analogy: AI infrastructure is draining capital from crypto the way productive Silicon Valley tech sucked capital out of overlevered, oversupplied gold miners after gold's 2011-12 blowoff top.
insights.speaker: guest, former gold-fund PM
insights.so_what: Capital rotates to the fastest-growing productive theme; crypto may keep bleeding allocation to AI infra until growth rates converge.
insights.quote: I'm almost wondering if we're watching the same thing happening in, you know, AI infrastructure to crypto

insights.claim: The administration used crypto as election leverage but has delivered little beyond drone-financing talk, ultimately backing the centralized banking industry over decentralization.
insights.speaker: guest, former gold-fund PM
insights.so_what: Don't underwrite a friendly-regulation crypto tailwind from this administration; policy favors incumbent banks.
insights.quote: it's clear the administration used crypto as a a leverage to, you know, for the for the election. And he hasn't they haven't really done anything

insights.claim: 99% of the token landscape is dismissed as 'nothing useful,' with even the majors corrupted by Ponzi-like Treasury-company market structure.
insights.speaker: guest, former gold-fund PM
insights.so_what: Broad altcoin exposure is viewed as worthless; concentration risk in even BTC/ETH from DAT structure is a hazard.
insights.quote: there's nothing useful uh in 99% of the token landscape

themes.theme: Iran oil shock, consumer health & stagflation risk

insights.claim: US crude inventories have fallen below the 5-year range and the SPR is being drawn toward zero to suppress oil prices, removing the buffer that made the Iran/Strait-of-Hormuz shock manageable so far.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: With buffers exhausted, oil-price suppression becomes harder — rising stagflation/oil-spike risk over coming months.
insights.quote: we're like like we're below the 5year range in inventories. I think I think now it gets a bit more real

insights.claim: Personal incomes are trending negative for the first time since 2022 and consumers are tapping savings to absorb the energy shock; historically negative real income episodes coincided with growth shocks or recessions like 2008.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: Negative income momentum plus energy shock skews toward a stagflationary growth scare — bearish the consumer and cyclicals.
insights.quote: incomes are actually negative for like the first time since 2022... it's it's during times of either growth shocks or outright recessions like '08

insights.claim: Consumer buffers (inventories, SPR, savings, and the big-beautiful-bill tax refunds) are all running out over the next couple months, so absent an Iran resolution things get 'pretty hairy' in a stagflationary way.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: Sets a near-term clock: monitor the next 1-2 months and an Iran deal as the key catalyst for a stagflation outcome.
insights.quote: all that stuff is running out over the next couple months. And so it's like we need that resolution or... things are going to get pretty hairy

insights.claim: Trump is expected to cut an Iran deal — likely 'worse than Obama's' — because he can't risk another months-long conflict with elevated gas prices four months before the midterms; today's headline was a classic trial balloon.
insights.speaker: guest, former gold-fund PM
insights.so_what: A de-escalation deal would relieve oil and risk; fade headline-driven oil spikes as politically-pressured resolution looms.
insights.quote: I think Trump's going to cut a deal that will probably end up being worse than Obama's... we're four months away from the midterms

insights.claim: Energy supply shocks are a 'nightmare' for central banks because they push headline inflation up at the same time real incomes turn negative — the exact bind constraining the Fed now.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: This bind keeps the Fed paralyzed (neither hiking nor easing decisively), reinforcing the no-hike, repression base case.
insights.quote: energy supply shocks are such a nightmare for central banks is that you have the supply shock in terms of headline inflation going higher... incomes are actually negative

themes.theme: K-shaped economy, passive flows & market structure

insights.claim: The K-shaped divergence is framed as a deliberate policy choice: propping equities and the long end via manipulation comes at the direct expense of Main Street, the opposite of letting market pain slow inflation and enable rate cuts that help lower-income households.
insights.speaker: guest, former gold-fund PM
insights.so_what: Policy that favors asset-holders entrenches asset inflation but builds political backlash — long assets short-term, hedge the regime long-term.
insights.quote: It is at the direct like literal direct expense of main street small business lower and middle inome class people

insights.claim: Mike Green's passive-investing thesis: the shift from defined-benefit pensions (managed with duration/liability hedging that kept markets rational) to defined-contribution plans turned every worker into a price-insensitive passive ETF buyer, making multi-trillion-dollar flows that no longer care about valuation.
insights.speaker: guest (Felix/Quinn)
insights.so_what: Price-insensitive passive flows are the structural bid under equities — explains valuation indifference and the melt-up's durability.
insights.quote: we basically manded every mandated every employee to become their own stock picker... They just buy passive ETFs every every paycheck

insights.claim: Inherited/wealthy capital compounds far better because it can withstand drawdowns, while lower-income investors can't take risk and fall further behind — a self-reinforcing erosion of the social contract.
insights.speaker: guest (Felix/Quinn) citing Mike Green
insights.so_what: Structural inequality dynamic underpins both the redistribution/political risk and the retail-leverage behavior driving markets.
insights.quote: your ability to compound that wealth is like exponentially better because you can deal with draw downs

insights.claim: Priced out of the centralized, asset-inflated system, younger investors are forced into high-risk behavior — 40x leverage on Hyperliquid and YOLO call options — as the only way to keep up.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: Explains the structural growth of leveraged retail flows and gamma dynamics; a fragility that can unwind violently.
insights.quote: I'm going to hyperlquid and yoloing 40x leverage or I'm going into call options. It's the only way you can stay up

insights.claim: Peter Thiel relocating to Argentina (on top of New Zealand bunkers) is read as an early, high-conviction signal of a US secular decline/socialism shift worth respecting rather than fading, given Thiel's track record.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: A prescient insider exiting US risk is a soft signal to watch for a deeper secular shift in US asset/political risk.
insights.quote: I do recognize him as a very powerful signal early to these secular shifts... that's more of like a signal of of the beginning

insights.claim: Mark Hart's thematic-valuation formula scores assets on access, awareness, patina (gravitas), TAM/right-tail growth, and use-as-collateral; new 'concentric circles of adoption' (ETF launch, custody solutions, ability to borrow against it) open fresh investor pools and signal entry/exit.
insights.speaker: guest, former gold-fund PM (ex-Mark Hart)
insights.so_what: A reusable lens: buy when an access/custody/collateral change widens the buyer base; sell when regulation restricts access.
insights.quote: he calls them concentric circles of adoption when you have access open up like take Bitcoin... like an an ETF, right? That's an access change

insights.claim: Regulatory or access restrictions (e.g., a Clarity Act outcome that limits access) are red flags most fundamental investors ignore because they only watch earnings — and they signal it's time to get out.
insights.speaker: guest, former gold-fund PM
insights.so_what: Track access/custody/collateral changes as leading exit signals, separate from earnings analysis.
insights.quote: those things are like red flags that most fundamental investors don't think about. They're just like, 'Oh, did the earnings go up?'

insights.claim: Best risk-adjusted entries into high-beta thematic trades come from buying pullbacks into a moving average within a multi-year bull market — before the theme reaches the front page of the WSJ — which removes stress and lets you play with house money.
insights.speaker: guest (Felix/Quinn)
insights.so_what: Entry quality dominates in volatile high-beta themes; chasing breakouts in fear yields poor entries and whipsaws.
insights.quote: buying this thing that's pulled back into a moving average in a multi-year bull market that's, you know, not yet the front page of the Wall Street Journal

insights.claim: Cash is a position and the discipline to sit out low-conviction, heavy-policy-influence environments — pressing hard only when edge is clear — beats forcing trades, since most P&L is made in short stretches.
insights.speaker: guest (Felix/Quinn)
insights.so_what: Process edge: hold cash and wait for clear setups rather than overtrading a policy-distorted tape.
insights.quote: cash is a position and if things don't make sense to you... I'm not going to force it

insights.claim: The panel separates trades by horizon — 1-3 month macro swings (e.g., a bearish Mag 7 trade, bullish metals) vs. 2-year secular holds (solar, semis) — using different trackers/accounts so short-term tactics don't sabotage long-term theses.
insights.speaker: guest, former gold-fund PM
insights.so_what: Book-segmentation by horizon prevents over-trading conviction themes — a practical risk-management structure.
insights.quote: the majority of my my trades and views and positions are one to three month swings... that's very different from like, hey, I'm super bullish this trend for two years

insights.claim: Named missed/winning thematic trades cited: Rocket Lab (called weekly for two years), Bitcoin-miners pivoting to AI data centers (recorded an hour on it in late 2024), Nebius (Leo's new biggest position), AI memory stocks, and SpaceX with an IPO ~4 months out as a hype catalyst.
insights.speaker: host (Tyler)
insights.so_what: Concrete AI/space themes flagged as the productive-asset winners; SpaceX pre-IPO hype and Nebius/AI-memory are watchlist names.
insights.quote: today Leo pulled his new biggest position was Nebius... you have the SpaceX IPO four months out... there's going to be hype going into it
